name: Poland
tagline: A thousand years between the great powers, surviving partition and conquest to rise again.
summary: Poland's history is one of a nation that has repeatedly been erased from the map and remade by the will of its people. The Polish state emerged in the tenth century when the Piast duke Mieszko I accepted Christianity in 966, binding his realm to Latin Europe. Medieval Piast and Jagiellonian kings forged a powerful kingdom that, joined to Lithuania, became the vast Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, one of early modern Europe's largest states and an experiment in noble republicanism. Decline, foreign interference and the paralysis of its own institutions led to the partitions of 1772, 1793 and 1795, when Russia, Prussia and Austria divided Poland out of existence for 123 years. Independence was restored in 1918, only for the Second Republic to be crushed in 1939 by Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union; the German occupation made Polish soil the site of the Holocaust and of staggering civilian death. Liberated into Soviet domination, Poland endured four decades of communist rule until the Solidarity movement helped bring it down, and the country returned to democracy, joining the European Union in 2004.

founding.label: The Baptism of Poland under Mieszko I
founding.detail: The conventional birth of the Polish state is dated to 966, when Mieszko I, duke of the Polanie and first historical ruler of the Piast dynasty, accepted Christianity from the Latin Church. The baptism, traditionally placed on 14 April 966, brought his realm into Western Christendom, helped shield it from forced conversion and absorption by the Holy Roman Empire, and is regarded as the founding act of the Polish nation. The state Mieszko consolidated, the Civitas Schinesghe, was the kernel of a kingdom that would endure in one form or another for a millennium. That continuity was shattered by the partitions of 1772, 1793 and 1795, which wiped Poland from the map for 123 years, until independence was restored on 11 November 1918 in the wake of the First World War.

quickFacts.label: Birth of the state
quickFacts.value: Baptism of Mieszko I, 966 (the Christianization of Poland)

quickFacts.label: First crowned king
quickFacts.value: Boleslaw I the Brave, crowned at Gniezno in 1025

quickFacts.label: Commonwealth founded
quickFacts.value: Union of Lublin joined Poland and Lithuania, 1 July 1569

quickFacts.label: Partitioned out of existence
quickFacts.value: Three partitions by Russia, Prussia and Austria: 1772, 1793, 1795

quickFacts.label: European Union
quickFacts.value: Joined the EU on 1 May 2004 after the fall of communism

eras.title: The Birth of the Piast State and the Christianization of Poland
eras.standfirst: A duke of the Polanie embraced Latin Christianity and bound a scattering of Slavic tribes into a state that would bear the name of Poland.
body: In the middle of the tenth century the lands between the Oder and the Vistula were held by a cluster of West Slavic tribes, among them the Polanie, the 'people of the fields', whose dukes of the Piast line gradually extended their dominion over their neighbours. The first of these rulers to emerge clearly into recorded history is Mieszko I, who by around 960 governed a substantial realm later described in a papal document as the Civitas Schinesghe.

Mieszko's decisive act was religious and political at once. In 966, having married the Christian Bohemian princess Dobrawa, he accepted baptism in the Latin rite. The conversion drew Poland into the orbit of Western, rather than Byzantine, Christendom, gave its rulers a recognised place among the Christian princes of Europe, and helped to forestall the kind of forced Christianization and conquest that Germany imposed elsewhere on the Slavs. In 1000 the emperor Otto III made a celebrated pilgrimage to Gniezno, where an independent Polish ecclesiastical province was established, signalling the new kingdom's standing.

Mieszko's son, Boleslaw I the Brave, expanded the state and in 1025 secured a royal crown, becoming the first king of Poland. The early kingdom remained fragile, however, dependent on the strength of individual rulers. When Boleslaw III Wrymouth died in 1138 he divided his realm among his sons in a testament intended to prevent succession wars; instead it inaugurated nearly two centuries of fragmentation into competing duchies, weakening Poland against its neighbours.

events.title: The Baptism of Poland
events.summary: Duke Mieszko I accepted Christianity in the Latin rite, conventionally dated to 14 April 966, bringing his realm into Western Christendom and marking the traditional birth of the Polish state.

events.title: Congress of Gniezno
events.summary: Emperor Otto III made pilgrimage to Gniezno and an independent Polish church province was established, recognising the young state's standing within Christian Europe.

events.title: Coronation of Boleslaw I the Brave
events.summary: Boleslaw I was crowned king at Gniezno, the first ruler to bear the royal title of Poland and to win recognition of the realm as a kingdom rather than a duchy.

events.title: Testament of Boleslaw III and fragmentation
events.summary: The dying Boleslaw III Wrymouth divided Poland among his sons, beginning nearly two centuries of fragmentation into rival duchies that weakened the state against its neighbours.

figures.name: Mieszko I
figures.blurb: The founder of the Polish state, Mieszko I united the lands of the Polanie and accepted Christianity in 966, anchoring Poland to Latin Europe. By his death his realm stretched from the Baltic toward the Carpathians.

figures.name: Boleslaw I the Brave
figures.blurb: Son of Mieszko I, Boleslaw expanded the kingdom in every direction and in 1025 obtained a royal crown, raising Poland to the rank of a kingdom shortly before his death.

pullquote.text: The baptism of Mieszko I in 966 was the true beginning of Polish history, joining the new state to the civilization of the Latin West.

eras.title: The Piast and Jagiellonian Kingdom
eras.standfirst: Reunified by the last great Piast kings and joined to Lithuania through the marriage of a queen, Poland became a major power of late medieval Europe.
body: The fragmentation that followed 1138 left Poland exposed to the Mongol invasions of the thirteenth century and to the aggressive expansion of the Teutonic Knights along the Baltic. Reunification came slowly. Wladyslaw I the Elbow-high reclaimed the royal crown in 1320, and his son Casimir III the Great (reigned 1333-1370) consolidated and transformed the realm. Remembered as the king who 'found a Poland of wood and left one of stone', Casimir codified the law, fortified towns, welcomed Jewish settlers fleeing persecution elsewhere, and in 1364 founded the university at Krakow, the second oldest in central Europe. He was the last of the Piast kings.

Casimir's death without a male heir opened the way to a new and momentous union. In 1385 the agreement at Krewo arranged the marriage of the young Polish monarch Jadwiga to Jogaila, grand duke of Lithuania, who was baptised, took the name Wladyslaw II Jagiello and accepted the Polish crown. The marriage joined the kingdom of Poland to the vast Grand Duchy of Lithuania and founded the Jagiellonian dynasty, under which a Polish-Lithuanian state stretched from the Baltic toward the Black Sea.

The new partnership soon faced the Teutonic Order. On 15 July 1410 the combined Polish and Lithuanian armies under Jagiello won a crushing victory over the Knights at the Battle of Grunwald, one of the largest battles of medieval Europe, breaking the Order's power in the Baltic. Over the fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries the Jagiellonian realm became a centre of Renaissance learning and religious tolerance, its astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus reshaping the understanding of the cosmos, its nobility steadily acquiring the privileges that would define the coming Commonwealth.

events.title: Reign of Casimir III the Great
events.summary: The last Piast king reformed the law, founded the University of Krakow in 1364, expanded the kingdom and welcomed Jewish settlers, leaving Poland prosperous and consolidated.

events.title: Union of Krewo and the Jagiellonian dynasty
events.summary: The agreement at Krewo arranged the marriage of Queen Jadwiga to Jogaila of Lithuania, who was baptised as Wladyslaw II Jagiello, joining Poland and Lithuania under a new dynasty.

events.title: Battle of Grunwald
events.summary: Polish and Lithuanian forces under Wladyslaw II Jagiello destroyed the army of the Teutonic Knights at Grunwald, halting the Order's eastward expansion in the Baltic.

events.title: Copernicus publishes 'On the Revolutions'
events.summary: The Polish astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus published his heliocentric theory, placing the Sun rather than the Earth at the centre of the cosmos and helping to launch modern astronomy.

figures.name: Casimir III the Great
figures.blurb: The only Polish king honoured as 'the Great', Casimir reformed law and administration, founded the University of Krakow, fortified the realm and extended protection to Jewish communities, leaving a stronger and wealthier kingdom.

figures.name: Nicolaus Copernicus
figures.blurb: Born in Torun in royal Prussia, Copernicus formulated the heliocentric model of the solar system, published in 1543, a turning point in the history of science and one of Poland's foremost contributions to world culture.

eras.title: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth: Golden Age and Decline
eras.standfirst: A noble republic of two nations rose to dominate eastern Europe, pioneered religious tolerance and a written constitution, then was paralysed from within and partitioned out of existence.
body: On 1 July 1569 the Union of Lublin transformed the dynastic link between Poland and Lithuania into a single federal state, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, the 'Republic of Both Nations'. Ruled by a monarch elected by the nobility and governed through a common parliament, the Sejm, the Commonwealth was one of the largest and most populous states in Europe. Its political culture, in which the szlachta, the noble class, held extensive rights, has been called a 'noble democracy'; its Warsaw Confederation of 1573 guaranteed an unusual measure of religious toleration at a time when much of Europe was convulsed by religious war.

The seventeenth century brought both glory and ruin. Polish and allied forces under King John III Sobieski relieved the Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683, a celebrated victory for Christian Europe. But the same century saw the Cossack uprising, wars with Muscovy and the catastrophic Swedish invasion of 1655-1660 known as 'the Deluge', which devastated the country. Internally, the Commonwealth was increasingly crippled by the liberum veto, the rule by which a single deputy could dissolve the Sejm and annul its work, allowing reform to be blocked and foreign powers to buy paralysis.

By the eighteenth century a weakened Poland had become a field for the ambitions of its neighbours. The first partition of 1772 by Russia, Prussia and Austria stripped away large territories. In a final burst of reform the Sejm adopted the Constitution of 3 May 1791, Europe's first modern codified constitution and the second in the world, seeking to strengthen the state and abolish the worst abuses. The reforms provoked Russian and Prussian intervention: the second partition followed in 1793, and after the defeat of Tadeusz Kosciuszko's national uprising the third partition of 1795 erased the Commonwealth entirely from the map of Europe.

events.title: Union of Lublin
events.summary: Poland and the Grand Duchy of Lithuania united into a single federal state, the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, ruled by an elected monarch and a common Sejm, one of Europe's largest powers.

events.title: Relief of Vienna
events.summary: King John III Sobieski led the allied cavalry charge that broke the Ottoman siege of Vienna, marking the high point of the Commonwealth's military reputation in Europe.

events.title: The Deluge
events.summary: A devastating Swedish invasion during the Second Northern War ravaged the Commonwealth, causing enormous loss of life and population and accelerating the state's long decline.

events.title: The Partitions of Poland
events.summary: In three stages (1772, 1793, 1795) Russia, Prussia and Austria divided the Commonwealth among themselves, despite the reforming Constitution of 3 May 1791, ending Polish statehood for 123 years.

figures.name: John III Sobieski
figures.role: King of Poland and military commander
figures.blurb: Elected king in 1674, Sobieski was the Commonwealth's most celebrated soldier-monarch, remembered above all for leading the relief of Vienna in 1683 against the Ottoman army.

figures.name: Tadeusz Kosciuszko
figures.blurb: A veteran of the American Revolutionary War, Kosciuszko led the 1794 national uprising against partition. Its defeat precipitated the final partition of 1795; he became an enduring symbol of the struggle for Polish freedom.

pullquote.text: The Constitution of 3 May 1791 was the first modern written constitution in Europe and the second in the world, adopted just as Poland was being torn apart.
pullquote.attribution: On the reforms that preceded the partitions

eras.title: The Partitions and the Long Fight for Independence
eras.standfirst: For 123 years no Polish state existed, yet through risings, exile and culture the idea of Poland was kept alive until the First World War made independence possible again.
body: After 1795 the Polish lands were ruled from Saint Petersburg, Berlin and Vienna. The Napoleonic era briefly raised hopes: the Duchy of Warsaw (1807-1815) gave a measure of self-government, but the Congress of Vienna in 1815 redrew the partition, creating a nominal Kingdom of Poland under the Russian tsar. Polish resistance erupted repeatedly, most notably in the November Uprising of 1830-1831 and the January Uprising of 1863-1864, both of which were crushed and followed by harsh repression, confiscations and intensified policies of Russification and Germanization.

Statelessness did not extinguish national identity; in many ways it sharpened it. Denied political institutions, Poles invested their nationhood in language, religion and culture. The poetry of Adam Mickiewicz, the music of Frederic Chopin, written largely in exile, and the Catholic Church all became vessels of a national consciousness that the partitioning powers could not suppress. Émigré communities, especially in France, sustained the cause abroad, while at home clandestine education and economic self-organisation preserved a sense of common purpose.

The First World War shattered the partitioning empires that had divided Poland. Russia, Germany and Austria-Hungary fought one another across Polish soil and all three collapsed by 1918. As the war ended, Polish leaders moved to restore the state: on 11 November 1918 Jozef Pilsudski took command in Warsaw and an independent Poland was proclaimed, reborn after more than a century of partition.

events.title: Congress of Vienna and the 'Congress Kingdom'
events.summary: The post-Napoleonic settlement re-divided the Polish lands and created a nominally autonomous Kingdom of Poland under the Russian tsar, entrenching foreign rule.

events.title: The November Uprising
events.summary: Poles in the Russian partition rose against the tsar; after initial successes the rising was crushed, bringing reprisals, the end of the Kingdom's autonomy and a wave of political exile.

events.title: The January Uprising
events.summary: A protracted guerrilla insurrection against Russian rule ended in defeat and severe repression, including executions, deportations to Siberia and intensified Russification.

events.title: Restoration of independence
events.summary: As the partitioning empires collapsed at the end of the First World War, an independent Polish state was proclaimed under Jozef Pilsudski, ending 123 years without statehood.

figures.name: Frederic Chopin
figures.blurb: Born near Warsaw and exiled to Paris after the November Uprising, Chopin poured a Polish national feeling into his music. His polonaises and mazurkas became enduring emblems of a nation that had lost its state.

figures.name: Jozef Pilsudski
figures.blurb: A socialist and conspirator turned military commander, Pilsudski organised Polish legions during the First World War and became the central figure of the reborn state, taking power on 11 November 1918.

pullquote.text: Though no sovereign Polish state existed between 1795 and 1918, the idea of Polish independence was kept alive throughout the nineteenth century.

eras.title: The Second Republic, the Second World War and the Nazi-Soviet Catastrophe
eras.standfirst: Barely two decades of restored independence ended in the joint German and Soviet invasion of 1939, an occupation of unparalleled brutality and the destruction of Poland's Jewish community in the Holocaust.
body: The Second Polish Republic of 1918-1939 was born into difficulty, knitting together territories that had been ruled for over a century by three different empires. It fought to secure its borders, repelling the Red Army at the Battle of Warsaw in 1920, and grappled with economic hardship and political instability, including the authoritarian turn after Pilsudski's coup of 1926. Yet it rebuilt a Polish state, educational system and culture, and was home to the largest Jewish community in Europe, some three million people.

That fragile independence was destroyed in 1939. On 1 September Nazi Germany invaded from the west, beginning the Second World War in Europe; on 17 September the Soviet Union invaded from the east, in accordance with the secret protocol of the German-Soviet pact of August 1939. Poland was overrun and partitioned once more between the two powers. The occupation that followed was among the most savage of the war. Germany sought to destroy the Polish nation as a political and cultural entity, murdering intellectuals, clergy and leaders, and treating the population as a source of forced labour.

Occupied Polish soil became the principal site of the Holocaust. Germany established its largest extermination camp at Auschwitz-Birkenau, where some 1.1 million people, the great majority of them Jews, were murdered, alongside other death camps such as Treblinka, Belzec and Sobibor. The Warsaw Ghetto Uprising of 1943 and the broader Warsaw Uprising of 1944, in which the Home Army rose against the Germans only to be crushed while the Soviet army halted nearby, became symbols of resistance and of catastrophe. By 1945 Germany had murdered roughly three million Polish Jews and between 1.8 and 1.9 million non-Jewish Polish civilians; the country had lost about a fifth of its population and its capital lay in ruins.

events.title: Battle of Warsaw
events.summary: Polish forces under Pilsudski halted the advance of the Soviet Red Army outside Warsaw, securing the independence of the young republic in the Polish-Soviet War.

events.title: German and Soviet invasion of Poland
events.summary: Nazi Germany invaded on 1 September, starting the Second World War in Europe; the Soviet Union invaded from the east on 17 September, and the two powers again partitioned Poland.

events.title: Auschwitz-Birkenau and the Holocaust on Polish soil
events.summary: Germany operated its largest concentration and extermination camp at Auschwitz-Birkenau, where some 1.1 million people, overwhelmingly Jews, were murdered as part of the genocide carried out across occupied Poland.

events.title: The Warsaw Uprising
events.summary: The Polish Home Army rose against the German occupiers in Warsaw; after 63 days the rising was crushed, much of the city was destroyed and tens of thousands of civilians were killed.

figures.name: Janusz Korczak
figures.blurb: Director of a Jewish orphanage in Warsaw, Korczak refused offers of his own safety and accompanied the children of his orphanage to their deaths at the Treblinka extermination camp, becoming a symbol of moral courage under the occupation.

figures.name: Witold Pilecki
figures.blurb: A cavalry officer of the Polish underground, Pilecki deliberately allowed himself to be imprisoned in Auschwitz to gather intelligence and organise resistance, later compiling some of the earliest detailed reports on the camp. He was executed by Poland's communist regime after the war.

pullquote.text: The Germans murdered at least three million Jewish citizens of Poland and between 1.8 and 1.9 million non-Jewish Polish civilians during the Second World War.

eras.title: Communist Poland, Solidarity and the Modern Republic
eras.standfirst: Liberated from German occupation into Soviet domination, Poland endured four decades of communist rule before Solidarity helped bring the system down and restored a democratic, European Poland.
body: The defeat of Germany did not bring Poland freedom but a new subjection. Liberated by the Red Army, the country was drawn into the Soviet sphere; a communist government, backed by Moscow and consolidated through rigged elections, established the Polish People's Republic. Its borders were shifted westward at the insistence of the Allies, with the loss of eastern territories to the Soviet Union and the acquisition of former German lands in the west, accompanied by vast forced movements of population. For more than four decades Poland was a one-party state within the Soviet bloc.

Resistance to communist rule recurred, in workers' protests in 1956, 1970 and 1976, and drew strength from the Catholic Church, above all after the election in 1978 of the Krakow archbishop Karol Wojtyla as Pope John Paul II. In 1980 a wave of strikes centred on the Gdansk shipyard produced Solidarity (Solidarnosc), the first independent, self-governing trade union in the Soviet bloc, led by the electrician Lech Walesa; within weeks it counted some ten million members. The regime responded with martial law in December 1981, outlawing the union and interning its leaders, but Solidarity survived underground.

The exhaustion of the communist system brought it to the negotiating table. The Round Table talks of 1989 led to partly free elections on 4 June 1989, the first of their kind in the Soviet bloc, which Solidarity won overwhelmingly and which helped set off the collapse of communism across Eastern Europe. Poland built a democratic republic and a market economy, joined NATO in 1999 and the European Union on 1 May 2004, becoming one of the larger member states and, after the hardships of transition, one of central Europe's notable economic successes.

events.title: Establishment of communist rule
events.summary: Backed by the Soviet Union and rigged elections, a communist government consolidated power, founding the Polish People's Republic within the Soviet bloc.

events.title: Birth of Solidarity
events.summary: Strikes at the Gdansk shipyard led by Lech Walesa produced Solidarity, the first independent trade union in the Soviet bloc, which rapidly grew to about ten million members.

events.title: Martial law
events.summary: The communist authorities imposed martial law, banned Solidarity and interned its leaders, including Walesa, driving the movement underground for the rest of the decade.

events.title: Semi-free elections and the fall of communism
events.summary: Round Table talks produced partly free elections that Solidarity won overwhelmingly, ending communist rule in Poland and helping trigger the collapse of the Soviet bloc.

events.title: Accession to the European Union
events.summary: Poland joined the European Union, completing its return to the West after the fall of communism and accelerating its economic and political integration into Europe.

figures.name: Lech Walesa
figures.blurb: An electrician at the Gdansk shipyard, Walesa led the strikes that created Solidarity and became its enduring symbol. Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1983, he was elected president of a democratic Poland in 1990.

figures.name: Pope John Paul II
figures.blurb: Born Karol Wojtyla in Wadowice, he became archbishop of Krakow and in 1978 the first Polish pope. His pilgrimages home galvanised national hope and lent decisive moral support to the Solidarity movement.

pullquote.text: The partly free elections of June 1989 were the first of their kind in the Soviet bloc, and Solidarity's victory helped set off the fall of communism across Eastern Europe.
pullquote.attribution: On the end of communist Poland

figures.name: Mieszko I
figures.blurb: The first historical ruler of Poland, Mieszko I accepted Christianity in 966, an act regarded as the founding of the Polish nation and its entry into Latin Christendom.

figures.name: Wladyslaw II Jagiello
figures.role: King of Poland, founder of the Jagiellonian dynasty
figures.blurb: Grand duke of Lithuania who married Queen Jadwiga, converted to Christianity and united Poland and Lithuania, founding the dynasty that ruled the rising power for nearly two centuries and winning the Battle of Grunwald in 1410.

figures.name: Nicolaus Copernicus
figures.blurb: Polish astronomer whose heliocentric model of the solar system, published in 1543, transformed the understanding of the cosmos and stands among Poland's greatest contributions to world science.

figures.name: Lech Walesa
figures.role: Solidarity leader and President of Poland
figures.blurb: Leader of the Solidarity trade union and Nobel Peace laureate whose movement helped end communist rule; he became the first popularly elected president of post-communist Poland in 1990.

sources.label: Auschwitz Birkenau German Nazi Concentration and Extermination Camp (1940-1945)
